---
title: "The Determinant: Signed Volume and Invertibility"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Polygon, FancyArrowPatch
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / data
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # the highlighted vector / image
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # area / volume highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"
ORANGE = "#d97706"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the closing article of **Movement III —
> Matrices as Maps**. It assumes [GEOM-08 *Matrices as Linear Maps*] (a matrix is a map
> $\mathbf{x}\mapsto A\mathbf{x}$, and $A\mathbf{x}$ is a linear combination of $A$'s
> columns — equivalently, the columns are *where the standard axis vectors land*),
> [GEOM-09 *Matrix Multiplication as Composition*] ($AB$ means "do $B$, then $A$"), and
> [GEOM-11 *Solving $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$*] (a square map is invertible exactly when its
> columns are independent, and singular when they collapse). It leans lightly on the
> transpose from [GEOM-10] and on the rank language of [GEOM-12 *Rank and the Four
> Fundamental Subspaces*] ($\det\neq 0 \Leftrightarrow$ full rank). Its job is to answer
> one question — *what single number tells you whether a square map is invertible, and why
> is "the factor by which it scales volume" the right way to understand it?* It sets up the
> **characteristic polynomial** $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$ of [GEOM-14 *Eigenvalues and
> Eigenvectors*], and it plants the **change-of-variables Jacobian** hook that the
> Probability track collects in [PROB-18 *Linear Transforms of Random Vectors*]. It also
> issues a warning it will pay off at the end of the track: *do not use the determinant to
> test whether a matrix is numerically singular* — that is the condition number's job
> ([GEOM-26]).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Every square matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ carries a single number, its
**determinant** $\det A$, with a startlingly concrete meaning: it is the **signed factor
by which the map $\mathbf{x}\mapsto A\mathbf{x}$ scales volume**. Feed in the unit cube;
the image is a parallelepiped whose (signed) volume is exactly $\det A$. The sign records
whether the map preserves or flips orientation — whether a right-handed coordinate frame
stays right-handed. From this one picture everything follows, and we derive it that way,
not from a cofactor formula memorized in a sophomore course. A map is **invertible
exactly when $\det A\neq 0$**, because $\det A=0$ means the cube was flattened — squashed
into lower dimension, its volume annihilated, its information irretrievably lost. The
multiplicativity law $\det(AB)=\det A\,\det B$ is just "scaling volume twice multiplies the
factors." We derive the $2\times 2$ and $3\times 3$ formulas as special cases of the
volume picture, show how row operations change the determinant, prove $\det(A^\top)=\det A$,
and then deliver the honest practitioner's verdict: the determinant is a beautiful
*theoretical* invariant — it powers the change-of-variables formula for densities and
defines the characteristic polynomial — but it is a **terrible numerical test for
singularity**, and we explain exactly why and what to use instead. The single claim this
article defends is that **the determinant is one geometric quantity, signed volume scaling,
and every algebraic property it has is a sentence about volume.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The **determinant** of a square matrix $A$ is the **signed factor by which the linear map
$\mathbf{x}\mapsto A\mathbf{x}$ multiplies volume**. Take the unit cube (in 2-D, the unit
square; in $n$-D, the unit hypercube); its image under $A$ is a parallelepiped, and
$\det A$ is that parallelepiped's signed volume. The **magnitude** $|\det A|$ is the volume
scaling factor; the **sign** says whether the map preserves orientation ($+$) or flips it
like a mirror ($-$). Consequently:

- $\det A = 0$ **exactly when the map collapses space** — flattens the cube into a
  lower-dimensional sheet of zero volume — which is exactly when $A$ is **singular
  (non-invertible)**. A nonzero determinant means full rank, an inverse, a unique solution
  to $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$.
- $\det(AB)=\det A\,\det B$, because doing two maps scales volume by each factor in turn.
- $\det(A^{-1}) = 1/\det A$, because undoing a map must undo its volume scaling.

Every algebraic rule about determinants is a restatement of one of these geometric facts.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The cofactor expansions and the row-operation rules that
fill linear-algebra textbooks will look like a grab-bag of formulas; by the end you should
see each one as a *sentence about volume*. The headline — $\det A=0$ means the map crushed
space — is the bridge straight back to [GEOM-11]'s singular matrices and [GEOM-12]'s
rank-deficient ones, now given a number you can compute.

# Why we are doing this: the problem of detecting a collapse {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **given a square matrix, is the map it
defines reversible — and if so, by how much does it stretch or squeeze the space it acts
on?** Every definition and derivation below answers a piece of that.

## What the previous articles left open

By [GEOM-11], a square matrix $A$ either is **invertible** — every target $\mathbf{b}$ is
hit by exactly one input, the columns are independent, the map can be undone — or it is
**singular** — the columns are dependent, the map collapses some directions to zero, and
$A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has either no solution or infinitely many. By [GEOM-12], the
dividing line is *rank*: a square $n\times n$ matrix is invertible exactly when its rank is
the full $n$.

But rank, as [GEOM-12] showed, is computed by elimination or read off the SVD — there is no
*single arithmetic expression in the entries of $A$* that returns "invertible or not." For
a $2\times2$ matrix you may dimly remember the test "$ad-bc\neq 0$." Where does that come
from? Why that combination of entries and no other? And what is the analogue for $3\times3$,
for $n\times n$? The determinant is the answer: **one polynomial in the entries of $A$ that
is zero precisely when the matrix is singular.** This article's task is to find that
polynomial, and — crucially — to understand it, not just compute it.

## Where the *why* came from historically

The determinant predates the matrix. In the late 1600s and 1700s, mathematicians solving
systems of linear equations — Leibniz, then Cramer in 1750 — kept noticing that the
*solvability* of a system, and the formula for its solution, hinged on one particular
alternating sum of products of coefficients. Cramer's rule expressed each unknown as a
ratio of two such sums. The expression was discovered as **the thing in the denominator
that, when it vanishes, the system has no unique solution**. That is its origin: a
solvability detector.

The geometric meaning — signed volume — came later, crystallized by Cauchy (who in 1812
gave the word "déterminant" its modern sense and proved the product rule) and by the 19th
century's geometers. We will run history in reverse, because the geometry is the *why* that
makes every algebraic fact inevitable. We start from "how does this map change volume,"
and the solvability detector falls out: **a map that crushes volume to zero cannot be
undone.**

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, what is the determinant?"* It is the signed volume-scaling factor of the
linear map the matrix performs — the factor by which it inflates or deflates volume, with a
sign for whether it flips orientation. Everything else (the invertibility test, the product
rule, Cramer's rule, the Jacobian in change-of-variables) is a consequence of that one
sentence. If you can only remember one thing about determinants, remember *signed volume*,
and you can reconstruct the rest.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any formula, fix the picture. By [GEOM-08], a matrix $A$ is completely described by
**where it sends the standard basis vectors** — the unit arrows along each axis. In 2-D,
$\mathbf{e}_1=(1,0)$ lands at the first column of $A$ and $\mathbf{e}_2=(0,1)$ lands at the
second. The unit square spanned by $\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{e}_2$ — the square with corners
$(0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1)$, of area exactly $1$ — is therefore sent to the **parallelogram**
spanned by the two columns of $A$.

The determinant is the (signed) area of that image parallelogram. Since the square we
started with had area $1$, this area *is* the factor by which the map scaled area: a
parallelogram of area $3$ means "this map triples areas everywhere." @fig-unit-square shows
the move.

```{python}
#| label: fig-unit-square
#| fig-cap: "The determinant as area scaling, in 2-D. **Left:** the unit square (area $1$), spanned by $\\mathbf{e}_1=(1,0)$ and $\\mathbf{e}_2=(0,1)$. **Right:** its image under $A=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\\\0&1.5\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$. The two columns $(2,0)$ and $(1,1.5)$ span a parallelogram; its area is $\\det A = 2\\cdot 1.5 - 1\\cdot 0 = 3$. So this map triples every area in the plane — the unit square became a region of area $3$, and the same factor applies to *any* region."
A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [0.0, 1.5]])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))

# Left: unit square.
sq = Polygon([(0, 0), (1, 0), (1, 1), (0, 1)], closed=True,
             facecolor=ACCENT, alpha=0.25, edgecolor=ACCENT, lw=1.8)
axL.add_patch(sq)
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), (1, 0), color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{e}_1$", label_offset=(0.12, -0.18))
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), (0, 1), color=HILITE, label=r"$\mathbf{e}_2$", label_offset=(-0.22, 0.10))
axL.text(0.5, 0.5, "area $= 1$", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=11, color=ACCENT)
axL.set_xlim(-0.6, 3.0); axL.set_ylim(-0.6, 2.4); axL.set_aspect("equal")
axL.set_title("Unit square in the input plane")
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$")

# Right: image parallelogram.
c1, c2 = A[:, 0], A[:, 1]
para = Polygon([(0, 0), tuple(c1), tuple(c1 + c2), tuple(c2)], closed=True,
               facecolor=ACCENT, alpha=0.25, edgecolor=ACCENT, lw=1.8)
axR.add_patch(para)
draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), tuple(c1), color=VEC, label=r"$A\mathbf{e}_1$", label_offset=(0.18, -0.22))
draw_vec(axR, (0, 0), tuple(c2), color=HILITE, label=r"$A\mathbf{e}_2$", label_offset=(-0.30, 0.12))
axR.text(1.4, 0.8, r"area $= \mathrm{det}\,A = 3$", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=11, color=ACCENT)
axR.set_xlim(-0.6, 3.6); axR.set_ylim(-0.6, 2.4); axR.set_aspect("equal")
axR.set_title("Image: columns span a parallelogram")
axR.set_xlabel("$y_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$y_2$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Three features of this picture carry the entire article, so name them now.

**Why a sign.** Area, as a raw geometric quantity, is positive. But the determinant can be
negative, and the sign is not noise — it records **orientation**. Walking from
$A\mathbf{e}_1$ to $A\mathbf{e}_2$ is either a counterclockwise turn (orientation
preserved, like the original $\mathbf{e}_1\to\mathbf{e}_2$ turn) or a clockwise turn
(orientation flipped, as if the plane were reflected in a mirror). The determinant is
*positive* for the first, *negative* for the second. A matrix with $\det A<0$ contains a
flip; a pure rotation, which preserves handedness, has $\det>0$.

**Why zero means collapse.** If the two columns happen to be parallel — point along the
same line — the "parallelogram" they span is degenerate: a flat sliver of zero area. The
map has crushed the 2-D square onto a 1-D line. That is precisely the singular case of
[GEOM-11]: dependent columns, lost dimension, no inverse. **Zero area $=$ zero determinant
$=$ collapse $=$ singular.** @fig-collapse-2d makes the collapse visible.

```{python}
#| label: fig-collapse-2d
#| fig-cap: "The singular case. The map $A=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}2&1\\\\4&2\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$ has a second column $(1,2)$ that is exactly half the first column $(2,4)$ — both lie on the line through $(1,2)$. The image of the unit square is not a parallelogram but a flat segment of *zero* area: the plane has been crushed onto a line. Its determinant is $2\\cdot 2 - 1\\cdot 4 = 0$. A zero determinant is the algebraic signature of this geometric collapse, and it is exactly the condition under which the map cannot be inverted."
A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0], [4.0, 2.0]])
c1, c2 = A[:, 0], A[:, 1]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.4, 4.6))
# The "parallelogram" degenerates to a segment along the line through (1,2).
t = np.linspace(-0.2, 3.4, 50)
direction = np.array([1.0, 2.0]) / np.sqrt(5)
ax.plot(t * direction[0], t * direction[1], color=GREY, lw=1.2, ls="--",
        label="image line (the collapse)")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(c1), color=VEC, label=r"$A\mathbf{e}_1=(2,4)$",
         label_offset=(0.9, 0.1))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(c2), color=HILITE, label=r"$A\mathbf{e}_2=(1,2)$",
         label_offset=(1.0, -0.3))
ax.text(1.5, 0.4, r"area $= \mathrm{det}\,A = 0$", fontsize=11, color=ACCENT)
ax.set_xlim(-0.6, 4.2); ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 5.0); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_title("Parallel columns: the square is crushed to a line")
ax.set_xlabel("$y_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y_2$")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

**Why it is the same factor everywhere.** The map is *linear*, and linear maps scale *all*
areas by the same factor. We do not have to check the unit square specially: a region of
area $S$ becomes a region of area $|\det A|\cdot S$, whatever its shape, because any region
is built from tiny copies of the square and the map treats them all alike. This is the
property that lets the determinant double as the Jacobian factor in calculus (@sec-jacobian).

The whole of @sec-formal makes these three observations precise and turns them into
formulas. In $n$ dimensions the unit *hypercube* of volume $1$ is sent to a
*parallelepiped*, and $\det A$ is its signed $n$-dimensional volume; the same three facts
hold verbatim.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We proceed in four moves. First we pin down what "signed volume" must mean by listing the
properties any honest area/volume function must have (@sec-axioms). Second, we show those
properties *force* a unique formula and derive the $2\times2$ and $3\times3$ cases
explicitly (@sec-formulas). Third, we derive the headline algebraic laws — the product rule,
the invertibility criterion, the transpose rule, and the effect of row operations — *from
the volume picture* (@sec-laws). Fourth, we connect to the cofactor expansion that lets us
compute any size (@sec-cofactor).

## What signed volume must satisfy {#sec-axioms}

Rather than pull a formula out of the air, we ask: what does it *mean* to measure the signed
volume of the parallelepiped spanned by $n$ vectors? Write the determinant as a function of
the $n$ columns,
$$
D(\mathbf{a}_1,\mathbf{a}_2,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n),
$$
where $\mathbf{a}_j\in\mathbb{R}^n$ is the $j$-th column of $A$ — that is, $A\mathbf{e}_j$,
where $\mathbf{e}_j$ lands. Three properties capture exactly what "signed volume of the
spanned box" means, and we will see they pin $D$ down completely.

::: {#def-det-properties}
**Definition (the defining properties of signed volume).** The determinant is the unique
function $D$ of the $n$ columns satisfying:

1. **Normalization.** $D(\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n)=1$. The unit cube has volume $1$.
   (When the columns *are* the standard axes, the matrix is the identity $I$, and we are
   measuring the cube we declared to have volume $1$.)

2. **Multilinearity.** $D$ is linear in each column separately, holding the others fixed.
   Concretely, for the $j$-th column,
   $$
   D(\dots,\ \alpha\mathbf{u}+\beta\mathbf{v},\ \dots)
   = \alpha\,D(\dots,\mathbf{u},\dots) + \beta\,D(\dots,\mathbf{v},\dots).
   $$

3. **Alternating (antisymmetry).** Swapping any two columns flips the sign:
   $$
   D(\dots,\mathbf{a}_i,\dots,\mathbf{a}_j,\dots)
   = -\,D(\dots,\mathbf{a}_j,\dots,\mathbf{a}_i,\dots).
   $$
:::

Each property is a statement about volume; let us read them as such, because the article's
spine is that *the algebra is geometry*.

**Normalization** is just the choice of unit: the box on the standard axes has volume one,
the meter-stick of the whole theory.

**Multilinearity** is the scaling-and-adding behavior of volume along one edge. Doubling one
edge of a box doubles its volume — that is $D(\dots,2\mathbf{u},\dots)=2D(\dots,\mathbf{u},
\dots)$, the $\alpha$ part. The additivity part ($\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{v}$ in one slot splits
the volume into two pieces) is the statement, familiar from the shear pictures of [GEOM-08],
that gluing two boxes along a face adds their volumes. @fig-multilinear illustrates the
$\alpha$-scaling half, which is the one most people find intuitive on sight.

```{python}
#| label: fig-multilinear
#| fig-cap: "Multilinearity, the scaling half. Stretching one edge vector by a factor $\\alpha$ stretches the spanned parallelogram's area by exactly $\\alpha$ (the other edge is untouched). Here doubling the second column from $(1,1.5)$ to $(2,3)$ doubles the area from $3$ to $6$. Volume is linear in each edge separately — that is property 2 of @def-det-properties."
a1 = np.array([2.0, 0.0])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.0, 4.4))
for a2, col, alpha_lab, fc in [(np.array([1.0, 1.5]), HILITE, r"$\mathbf{a}_2$", 0.18),
                               (np.array([2.0, 3.0]), ORANGE, r"$2\mathbf{a}_2$", 0.12)]:
    para = Polygon([(0, 0), tuple(a1), tuple(a1 + a2), tuple(a2)], closed=True,
                   facecolor=fc and col, alpha=fc, edgecolor=col, lw=1.6)
    ax.add_patch(para)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), tuple(a1), color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{a}_1$", label_offset=(0.0, -0.3))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), (1.0, 1.5), color=HILITE)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), (2.0, 3.0), color=ORANGE)
ax.text(1.3, 0.5, "area 3", color=HILITE, fontsize=10)
ax.text(2.5, 1.6, "area 6", color=ORANGE, fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(-0.4, 4.6); ax.set_ylim(-0.4, 3.6); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_title("Scaling one edge scales the area by the same factor")
ax.set_xlabel("$y_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y_2$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

**The alternating property** is where the *sign* of "signed volume" lives, and it has one
consequence so important we extract it immediately.

::: {#lem-repeated-zero}
**Lemma (repeated column $\Rightarrow$ zero).** If two columns of $A$ are equal, then
$D(A)=0$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Suppose columns $i$ and $j$ are the same vector, $\mathbf{a}_i=\mathbf{a}_j=\mathbf{a}$.
Swapping those two columns leaves the matrix entirely unchanged — we swapped a vector with
its identical twin, and the resulting list of columns is the same list — so $D$ is unchanged:
$D(A)=D(A)$. But the alternating property
(property 3) says swapping columns $i$ and $j$ must *negate* $D$: $D(A)=-D(A)$. A number
equal to its own negative satisfies $D(A)=-D(A)$, i.e. $2D(A)=0$, so $D(A)=0$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Geometrically this is obvious and reassuring: if two edges of the box point in the same
direction, the box is flat — it has been squashed into a lower-dimensional sheet — so its
volume is zero. @lem-repeated-zero is the seed of the entire invertibility story: dependent
columns force the determinant to zero, which is the collapse we drew in @fig-collapse-2d. We
sharpen "two equal columns" to the full "any linear dependence among columns" in
@sec-laws.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why define the determinant by three abstract properties instead of just writing the
formula?"* Two reasons. First, the properties *are* the geometry — normalization is the
unit cube, multilinearity is "volume scales with each edge," antisymmetry is orientation —
so they explain the formula instead of asserting it. Second, the properties are what make
the *proofs* clean: the product rule $\det(AB)=\det A\det B$ and the row-operation rules
fall out in a couple of lines from properties 1–3, whereas grinding them out of the entry
formula is a mess. The formula is a *consequence*; the properties are the *definition*. This
"characterize by properties, then derive the formula" move is exactly how mathematicians
actually think about the determinant.
:::

## The properties force a unique formula: $2\times2$ and $3\times3$ {#sec-formulas}

We now show that properties 1–3 do not merely describe *a* volume function — they pin down
*the* formula, with no freedom left. We do the $2\times2$ case in full, because the same
mechanism produces every larger case.

### The $2\times2$ formula, derived

Let
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix},
\qquad
\mathbf{a}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} a \\ c \end{bmatrix},\quad
\mathbf{a}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} b \\ d \end{bmatrix},
$$
so the two columns are $\mathbf{a}_1=a\mathbf{e}_1+c\mathbf{e}_2$ and
$\mathbf{a}_2=b\mathbf{e}_1+d\mathbf{e}_2$, where $\mathbf{e}_1=(1,0)$ and
$\mathbf{e}_2=(0,1)$. Apply multilinearity to expand $D(\mathbf{a}_1,\mathbf{a}_2)$. First
expand in the first column (treating $\mathbf{a}_2$ as a fixed second slot):
$$
D(\mathbf{a}_1,\mathbf{a}_2)
= D(a\mathbf{e}_1 + c\mathbf{e}_2,\ \mathbf{a}_2)
= a\,D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{a}_2) + c\,D(\mathbf{e}_2,\mathbf{a}_2),
$$
using property 2 in the first slot. Now expand each of those in the second column,
$\mathbf{a}_2=b\mathbf{e}_1+d\mathbf{e}_2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{a}_2)
  &= D(\mathbf{e}_1,\ b\mathbf{e}_1 + d\mathbf{e}_2)
   = b\,D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{e}_1) + d\,D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{e}_2),\\
D(\mathbf{e}_2,\mathbf{a}_2)
  &= D(\mathbf{e}_2,\ b\mathbf{e}_1 + d\mathbf{e}_2)
   = b\,D(\mathbf{e}_2,\mathbf{e}_1) + d\,D(\mathbf{e}_2,\mathbf{e}_2).
\end{aligned}
$$
Substituting back, $D(\mathbf{a}_1,\mathbf{a}_2)$ is the sum of four terms:
$$
D(\mathbf{a}_1,\mathbf{a}_2)
= ab\,D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{e}_1)
+ ad\,D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{e}_2)
+ cb\,D(\mathbf{e}_2,\mathbf{e}_1)
+ cd\,D(\mathbf{e}_2,\mathbf{e}_2).
$$
Now evaluate the four basis determinants using properties 1 and 3, and @lem-repeated-zero:

- $D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{e}_1)=0$ and $D(\mathbf{e}_2,\mathbf{e}_2)=0$ by
  @lem-repeated-zero (a repeated column).
- $D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{e}_2)=1$ by normalization (these columns *are* the identity).
- $D(\mathbf{e}_2,\mathbf{e}_1)=-D(\mathbf{e}_1,\mathbf{e}_2)=-1$ by the alternating
  property (swap the two columns of the identity).

The first and last terms drop, and we are left with
$$
\boxed{\;
D(A) = ad\cdot 1 + cb\cdot(-1) = ad - bc.
\;}
$$ {#eq-det2}

There it is — the half-remembered "$ad-bc$," now *derived* rather than memorized, and seen
to be forced by the three volume properties with no choice along the way. The combination is
not arbitrary: it is the only multilinear, alternating, normalized function of the columns.

### The same expansion in general: the uniqueness lemma {#sec-uniqueness}

The $2\times2$ expansion was not a special trick — it was the *general* mechanism run at
$n=2$. We now run it at general $n$ once, because the result is the structural fact the
product rule and the transpose rule both stand on. Let $\Phi$ be **any** multilinear,
alternating function of $n$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ (we do not yet assume normalization).
Write each input column in the standard basis, $\mathbf{a}_j=\sum_{k=1}^{n}A_{kj}\mathbf{e}_k$,
where $A_{kj}$ is the $k$-th entry of the $j$-th column. Expand $\Phi$ by multilinearity in
every slot at once — each slot $j$ contributes its own summation index $k_j$:
$$
\Phi(\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n)
= \sum_{k_1=1}^{n}\cdots\sum_{k_n=1}^{n}
  A_{k_1 1}A_{k_2 2}\cdots A_{k_n n}\;
  \Phi(\mathbf{e}_{k_1},\mathbf{e}_{k_2},\dots,\mathbf{e}_{k_n}).
$$
This is just property 2 applied $n$ times, exactly as in the $2\times2$ case (which had two
nested sums of two terms each). Now use the alternating property to prune the $n^n$ terms.
By @lem-repeated-zero, $\Phi(\mathbf{e}_{k_1},\dots,\mathbf{e}_{k_n})=0$ whenever two of the
indices $k_1,\dots,k_n$ are equal. So the *only* surviving terms are those where
$(k_1,\dots,k_n)$ is a **permutation** of $(1,\dots,n)$ — call such an ordering $\pi$, with
$\pi(j)=k_j$. For each permutation $\pi$, the value $\Phi(\mathbf{e}_{\pi(1)},\dots,
\mathbf{e}_{\pi(n)})$ is the identity columns $\Phi(\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n)$ shuffled
into the order $\pi$. Antisymmetry (property 3) says each swap that sorts $\pi$ back to
$(1,2,\dots,n)$ flips the sign once, so
$$
\Phi(\mathbf{e}_{\pi(1)},\dots,\mathbf{e}_{\pi(n)})
= \operatorname{sgn}(\pi)\;\Phi(\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n),
$$
where $\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)=(-1)^{(\text{number of swaps to sort }\pi)}=+1$ for an even
number of swaps and $-1$ for an odd number. (This sign is well-defined — the *parity* of the
swap count does not depend on which swaps you choose — precisely because $\Phi(\mathbf{e}_1,
\dots,\mathbf{e}_n)$ would otherwise be forced to equal both $+$ and $-$ itself, and the only
number equal to its own negative is $0$; we exclude that by allowing $\Phi$ to be nonzero on
the identity.) Substituting back,
$$
\Phi(\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n)
= \Phi(\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n)\;
  \underbrace{\sum_{\pi}\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)\,
  A_{\pi(1)1}A_{\pi(2)2}\cdots A_{\pi(n)n}}_{\textstyle =\ \det A\ \text{(the Leibniz formula)}},
$$
the sum running over all $n!$ permutations $\pi$ of $\{1,\dots,n\}$. We package this as the
lemma the rest of the article cites.

::: {#lem-uniqueness}
**Lemma (uniqueness / the expansion formula).** Every multilinear, alternating function
$\Phi$ of $n$ columns in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a single constant times the determinant,
$$
\Phi(\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n)
= \Phi(\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n)\cdot\det A,
\qquad
\det A := \sum_{\pi}\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)\prod_{j=1}^{n}A_{\pi(j)\,j}.
$$
The free constant is $\Phi$'s value on the identity columns. Imposing **normalization**
($\Phi(\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n)=1$) fixes that constant to $1$, so there is **exactly
one** normalized multilinear alternating function — the determinant — and the displayed
permutation sum (the **Leibniz formula**) is its explicit form.
:::

For $n=2$ the two permutations are the identity ($\operatorname{sgn}=+1$, term $A_{11}A_{22}=
ad$) and the single swap ($\operatorname{sgn}=-1$, term $A_{21}A_{12}=cb$), recovering
$ad-bc$ — the same four-term expansion we did above, now seen as the $n=2$ instance of one
formula. This lemma is doing triple duty: it *defines* $\det$ for every size, it proves the
function is *unique*, and its "constant times $\det$" form is precisely what makes the product
rule (@thm-product) a two-line argument.

::: {#def-det}
**Definition (determinant).** The **determinant** of a square matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times
n}$, written $\det A$ or $|A|$, is the value $D(\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n)$ of the
unique multilinear, alternating, normalized function of its columns (@def-det-properties).
That function exists, is unique, and equals the permutation sum of @lem-uniqueness; for $n=2$
it equals $ad-bc$ by @eq-det2. The cofactor expansion of @sec-cofactor is the same number
regrouped for hand computation.
:::

### The $3\times3$ formula, by the same machine

Specialize @lem-uniqueness to $n=3$. Of the $3^3=27$ terms in the multilinear expansion, only
the ones whose index triple $(\pi(1),\pi(2),\pi(3))$ is a permutation of $(1,2,3)$ survive
@lem-repeated-zero — there are $3!=6$ of them, one per ordering. Listing the six permutations
of $(1,2,3)$ with their signs (identity and the two $3$-cycles are even, $\operatorname{sgn}=
+1$; the three single-swap transpositions are odd, $\operatorname{sgn}=-1$) and reading the
entry products $A_{\pi(1)1}A_{\pi(2)2}A_{\pi(3)3}$ off the matrix gives the familiar rule. For
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b & c \\ d & e & f \\ g & h & i \end{bmatrix},
$$
$$
\det A = aei + bfg + cdh - ceg - bdi - afh.
$$ {#eq-det3}
The three positive terms ($aei$, $bfg$, $cdh$) are the even permutations; the three negative
ones ($ceg$, $bdi$, $afh$) are the odd ones. We do not memorize @eq-det3; we
*recover* it from the expansion when needed, or — far more usefully — we reduce to it via
the cofactor recursion of @sec-cofactor. The point of showing it is to confirm the same
machine that gave $ad-bc$ scales to any size and always yields a single polynomial in the
entries.

The signed volume reading is identical: @eq-det3 is the signed volume of the parallelepiped
spanned by the three columns of $A$, which is the unit cube's image. @fig-parallelepiped
draws that 3-D box.

```{python}
#| label: fig-parallelepiped
#| fig-cap: "In 3-D, the unit cube (volume $1$) maps to the parallelepiped spanned by the three columns of $A$, and $\\det A$ is its signed volume. Here $A$ has columns $(1.6,0.2,0)$, $(0.4,1.3,0.1)$, $(0.2,0.3,1.4)$; the volume scaling factor is $\\det A\\approx 2.86$, so this map nearly triples 3-D volume. Flatten any column into the plane of the other two and the box collapses to zero thickness — that is $\\det A=0$, singular."
A = np.array([[1.6, 0.4, 0.2],
              [0.2, 1.3, 0.3],
              [0.0, 0.1, 1.4]])
c1, c2, c3 = A[:, 0], A[:, 1], A[:, 2]
O = np.zeros(3)
# Eight corners of the parallelepiped: O + combinations of the three columns.
corners = np.array([O, c1, c2, c3, c1 + c2, c1 + c3, c2 + c3, c1 + c2 + c3])
faces = [[O, c1, c1 + c2, c2], [O, c1, c1 + c3, c3], [O, c2, c2 + c3, c3],
         [c3, c1 + c3, c1 + c2 + c3, c2 + c3], [c1, c1 + c2, c1 + c2 + c3, c1 + c3],
         [c2, c1 + c2, c1 + c2 + c3, c2 + c3]]

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.2, 5.2))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
pc = Poly3DCollection(faces, facecolor=ACCENT, alpha=0.22, edgecolor=VEC, lw=1.4)
ax.add_collection3d(pc)
for c, lab, col in [(c1, r"$A\mathbf{e}_1$", VEC), (c2, r"$A\mathbf{e}_2$", HILITE),
                    (c3, r"$A\mathbf{e}_3$", ORANGE)]:
    ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, c[0], c[1], c[2], color=col, lw=2.4, arrow_length_ratio=0.12)
    ax.text(c[0], c[1], c[2], lab, color=col, fontsize=11)
ax.set_xlim(0, 2.2); ax.set_ylim(0, 2.2); ax.set_zlim(0, 2.2)
ax.set_xlabel("$y_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y_2$"); ax.set_zlabel("$y_3$")
ax.set_title(f"Image of the unit cube: volume = det A = {np.linalg.det(A):.2f}")
ax.view_init(elev=22, azim=-58)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The algebraic laws, derived from volume {#sec-laws}

We now harvest the payoff. Each law below is proved from properties 1–3 (equivalently, from
the volume picture) in a few lines — and each, read aloud, is a sentence about volume.

### Dependence among columns forces the determinant to zero

@lem-repeated-zero handled *equal* columns. The real statement we want is the full
invertibility criterion, so we upgrade it.

::: {#thm-singular}
**Theorem (zero determinant $\Leftrightarrow$ singular).** $\det A = 0$ if and only if the
columns of $A$ are linearly dependent — equivalently, $A$ is singular (non-invertible, rank
$< n$).
:::

::: {.proof}
**($\Leftarrow$) Dependence $\Rightarrow \det A=0$.** Suppose the columns are dependent.
Then (by the definition of dependence, [GEOM-03]) some column is a linear combination of the
others; relabel so it is the last, $\mathbf{a}_n = \sum_{j=1}^{n-1}c_j\mathbf{a}_j$. Expand
$\det A = D(\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_{n-1},\ \sum_j c_j\mathbf{a}_j)$ by multilinearity
in the last slot:
$$
\det A = \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} c_j\,D(\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_{n-1},\ \mathbf{a}_j).
$$
In each term of the sum, the vector $\mathbf{a}_j$ ($j\le n-1$) appears *twice* — once in
its original slot $j$ and once in the last slot. By @lem-repeated-zero every such term is
zero. Hence $\det A = 0$.

**($\Rightarrow$) $\det A = 0 \Rightarrow$ dependence.** We prove the contrapositive: if the
columns are *independent*, then $\det A\neq 0$. Independent columns of an $n\times n$ matrix
form a basis of $\mathbb{R}^n$ ([GEOM-03]: $n$ independent vectors in an $n$-dimensional space
are a basis), so the matrix is invertible ([GEOM-11]): there is a matrix $A^{-1}$ with
$A^{-1}A = I$. We will show in @thm-product below that $\det$ is multiplicative, giving
$\det(A^{-1})\det(A) = \det(A^{-1}A)=\det I = 1$ (normalization). A product equal to $1$
cannot have a zero factor, so $\det A\neq 0$. (The product rule's proof, @thm-product, uses
only properties 1–3 and does *not* use this theorem, so there is no circularity.)
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the headline of the article, now proved: **the determinant vanishes exactly when
the map collapses space.** The number $\det A$ is the long-sought single arithmetic test for
invertibility. Read against [GEOM-12]: $\det A\neq 0 \Leftrightarrow$ full rank
$\Leftrightarrow$ trivial null space $\Leftrightarrow$ the four-subspace picture degenerates
to "nothing is crushed."

### The product rule: scaling volume twice

::: {#thm-product}
**Theorem (multiplicativity).** For square $A,B$ of the same size,
$$
\det(AB) = \det A\,\det B .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The geometric idea is one sentence: the composite map $AB$ does $B$ first (scaling volume by
$\det B$) and then $A$ (scaling the already-scaled volume by $\det A$), so the total volume
factor is the product $\det A\,\det B$. We make this a proof using the uniqueness from
@sec-formulas.

Fix $A$ and define a function of the columns of $B$,
$$
\Phi(\mathbf{b}_1,\dots,\mathbf{b}_n) := \det(AB),
$$
where $\mathbf{b}_1,\dots,\mathbf{b}_n$ are the columns of $B$. Recall ([GEOM-09]) that the
$j$-th column of $AB$ is $A\mathbf{b}_j$. So $\Phi$ is the determinant of the matrix with
columns $A\mathbf{b}_1,\dots,A\mathbf{b}_n$. We check $\Phi$ is multilinear and alternating
*in the columns $\mathbf{b}_j$*:

- *Multilinear.* If $\mathbf{b}_j = \alpha\mathbf{u}+\beta\mathbf{v}$, then by linearity of
  the map $A$ ([GEOM-08]), $A\mathbf{b}_j = \alpha A\mathbf{u} + \beta A\mathbf{v}$. Since
  $\det$ is multilinear in *its* columns (property 2), $\Phi$ splits accordingly:
  $\Phi(\dots,\alpha\mathbf{u}+\beta\mathbf{v},\dots) = \alpha\Phi(\dots,\mathbf{u},\dots) +
  \beta\Phi(\dots,\mathbf{v},\dots)$.
- *Vanishes on repeats.* If $\mathbf{b}_i=\mathbf{b}_j$, then $A\mathbf{b}_i = A\mathbf{b}_j$,
  so the matrix $AB$ has two equal columns and $\det(AB)=0$ by @lem-repeated-zero. So $\Phi$
  vanishes whenever two of its arguments coincide.
- *Alternating.* A multilinear function that vanishes on repeats is automatically
  *alternating* (sign-flips on a swap), and here is the two-line derivation, not a cited fact.
  Hold all slots but $i$ and $j$ fixed, and feed the *same* vector $\mathbf{w}=\mathbf{u}+
  \mathbf{v}$ into both slots $i$ and $j$. Vanishing-on-repeats gives
  $\Phi(\dots,\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{v},\dots,\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{v},\dots)=0$. Expand the left side
  by multilinearity in slot $i$ and then in slot $j$ (four terms):
  $$
  \underbrace{\Phi(\dots,\mathbf{u},\dots,\mathbf{u},\dots)}_{=\,0\ \text{(repeat)}}
  + \Phi(\dots,\mathbf{u},\dots,\mathbf{v},\dots)
  + \Phi(\dots,\mathbf{v},\dots,\mathbf{u},\dots)
  + \underbrace{\Phi(\dots,\mathbf{v},\dots,\mathbf{v},\dots)}_{=\,0\ \text{(repeat)}} = 0 .
  $$
  The two underbraced terms vanish (each has a repeated argument), leaving
  $\Phi(\dots,\mathbf{u},\dots,\mathbf{v},\dots) = -\,\Phi(\dots,\mathbf{v},\dots,\mathbf{u},
  \dots)$ — which is exactly antisymmetry under swapping slots $i$ and $j$. So $\Phi$ is
  alternating.

So $\Phi$ is a multilinear alternating function of $n$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ — exactly the
hypothesis of @lem-uniqueness. The lemma therefore applies verbatim: $\Phi$ equals $\det$
times its value on the identity columns,
$$
\Phi(\mathbf{b}_1,\dots,\mathbf{b}_n) = \Phi(\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n)\cdot
\det\!\big([\mathbf{b}_1\ \cdots\ \mathbf{b}_n]\big).
$$
Here $\det\big([\mathbf{b}_1\ \cdots\ \mathbf{b}_n]\big)=\det B$, and the constant is
$$
\Phi(\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n) = \det(A\,[\mathbf{e}_1\ \cdots\ \mathbf{e}_n])
= \det(AI) = \det A .
$$
Therefore $\det(AB) = \Phi(\mathbf{b}_1,\dots,\mathbf{b}_n) = \det A\cdot\det B$, as claimed.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The product rule discharges several smaller facts at once.

- **Inverse.** From $A^{-1}A=I$ and the product rule, $\det(A^{-1})\det(A)=\det I = 1$, so
  $$
  \det(A^{-1}) = \frac{1}{\det A}.
  $$ {#eq-det-inv}
  An invertible map's volume factor must have a reciprocal — you cannot undo a $3\times$
  inflation except by a $\tfrac{1}{3}\times$ deflation. (This also re-confirms that an
  invertible matrix has $\det A\neq 0$, since $1/\det A$ must exist.)
- **Powers and similarity.** $\det(A^k)=(\det A)^k$, and for any invertible $P$,
  $\det(P^{-1}AP)=\det(P^{-1})\det A\det P = \tfrac{1}{\det P}\det A\,\det P = \det A$ — a
  *similarity transformation preserves the determinant*. This is exactly why the determinant
  is well-defined as a property of the underlying *map*, not the particular coordinates, and
  it is the fact [GEOM-14] uses when it builds the characteristic polynomial.

### The transpose rule

::: {#thm-transpose}
**Theorem (transpose).** $\det(A^\top) = \det A$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The general proof uses the Leibniz sum already derived in @lem-uniqueness,
$$
\det A = \sum_{\pi}\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)\prod_{j=1}^{n}A_{\pi(j)\,j},
$$
the sum over all permutations $\pi$ of $\{1,\dots,n\}$. Apply it to $A^\top$, whose entries
are $(A^\top)_{ij}=A_{ji}$:
$$
\det(A^\top) = \sum_{\pi}\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)\prod_{j=1}^{n}(A^\top)_{\pi(j)\,j}
            = \sum_{\pi}\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)\prod_{j=1}^{n}A_{j\,\pi(j)} .
$$
Now rewrite one generic product $\prod_{j}A_{j\,\pi(j)}$ in terms of the inverse permutation
$\sigma=\pi^{-1}$ (so $\sigma(\pi(j))=j$). The factors are the same $n$ numbers in a different
order: setting $i=\pi(j)$ runs $i$ over all of $\{1,\dots,n\}$ as $j$ does, and then $j=
\sigma(i)$, so $\prod_{j}A_{j\,\pi(j)}=\prod_{i}A_{\sigma(i)\,i}$. Two facts close it. First,
$\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)=\operatorname{sgn}(\pi^{-1})=\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)$: writing
$\pi$ as a sequence of transpositions and reversing that sequence builds $\pi^{-1}$ with the
*same number* of transpositions, so the parities match. Second, summing over all $\pi$ is the
same as summing over all $\sigma$, since inversion is a bijection of the set of permutations
onto itself. Therefore
$$
\det(A^\top) = \sum_{\pi}\operatorname{sgn}(\pi)\prod_{i}A_{\sigma(i)\,i}
            = \sum_{\sigma}\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\prod_{i}A_{\sigma(i)\,i}
            = \det A,
$$
the last sum being the Leibniz formula for $\det A$ verbatim. Hence $\det(A^\top)=\det A$.

We verify the claim concretely in the $2\times2$ case so it is not just a permutation
argument: with $A=\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$ we have
$A^\top=\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}a&c\\b&d\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$, and
$\det(A^\top)=ad-cb=ad-bc=\det A$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The geometric consequence is the one to carry: **everything we proved about columns holds
verbatim for rows.** The determinant is the signed volume of the box spanned by the *rows*
just as much as the columns, the row dependence test mirrors the column one, and the
cofactor expansion (next) may be done along any row or any column. The transpose rule is the
formal license for that symmetry.

### Row (and column) operations

The three elementary operations of Gaussian elimination ([GEOM-11]) each have a clean,
volume-flavored effect on the determinant. These are the *practical* way determinants are
computed, so we state and justify each from properties 1–3.

1. **Add a multiple of one row to another:** *determinant unchanged.* Adding
   $\lambda\,(\text{row }i)$ to row $k$ can be expanded by multilinearity in row $k$ into
   $\det A + \lambda\,D(\text{rows with row }i\text{ in slot }k\text{ too})$; the second
   determinant has row $i$ appearing twice and so is zero (@lem-repeated-zero, on rows via
   @thm-transpose). Net change: zero. *Geometrically: a shear slides the box parallel to a
   face and does not change its volume — base times height is unchanged.*

2. **Multiply a row by a scalar $\lambda$:** *determinant multiplied by $\lambda$.* This is
   multilinearity (property 2) applied to that row: scaling one edge of the box scales the
   volume by the same factor.

3. **Swap two rows:** *determinant changes sign.* This is the alternating property
   (property 3) on rows. *Geometrically: a swap of two edges reverses orientation.*

These rules turn computation into elimination: reduce $A$ to an upper-triangular matrix $U$
tracking the sign flips and scalings, and read $\det A$ off the diagonal of $U$, because the
determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of its diagonal entries (each off-diagonal
entry can be cleared by operation 1 without changing the determinant, leaving a diagonal
matrix whose determinant is the product of diagonal entries by repeated multilinearity and
normalization). This is how every numerical library actually computes a determinant — never
by the cofactor formula — and it costs the same $O(n^3)$ as solving a system.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How is a determinant computed in practice, and how expensive is it?"* Not by cofactor
expansion — that is $O(n!)$ and unusable past tiny $n$. In practice you do **LU
decomposition** (Gaussian elimination, [GEOM-11]): factor $A=PLU$ with $P$ a row-swap
(permutation) matrix, $L$ lower-triangular with unit diagonal, $U$ upper-triangular. Then
$\det A = \det P\cdot\det L\cdot\det U = (\pm 1)\cdot 1\cdot\prod_i U_{ii}$ — the product of
$U$'s diagonal, times $-1$ for each row swap. Cost: $O(n^3)$, the same as a linear solve.
The honest follow-up the interviewer wants (covered in @sec-failure): even computed this
well, the determinant is a *poor* way to decide invertibility numerically.
:::

## The cofactor expansion: computing any size {#sec-cofactor}

For completeness and hand-computation of small matrices, we record the recursive **cofactor
(Laplace) expansion**, which the Leibniz permutation sum of @lem-uniqueness produces when you
group its $n!$ terms by which entry of a single row (or column) they contain. It is not a new
definition — it is @def-det equivalently restated — and we show it is consistent with @eq-det2.

::: {#def-cofactor}
**Definition (minor and cofactor).** For $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$, the **minor** $M_{ij}$
is the determinant of the $(n-1)\times(n-1)$ matrix obtained by deleting row $i$ and column
$j$ from $A$. The **cofactor** is the signed minor $C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j}M_{ij}$.
:::

::: {#thm-cofactor}
**Theorem (Laplace expansion).** For any fixed row $i$,
$$
\det A = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\,C_{ij}
       = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (-1)^{i+j} A_{ij}\,M_{ij},
$$
and likewise along any fixed column $j$ (using @thm-transpose).
:::

We do not re-prove this from scratch — it *is* the Leibniz sum of @lem-uniqueness with its
$n!$ terms collected by the entries $A_{ij}$ of one fixed row $i$ (each group, after factoring
out $A_{ij}$, is a signed Leibniz sum over the remaining $(n-1)!$ permutations of the deleted
submatrix, which is exactly $\pm M_{ij}$, with the sign collapsing to $(-1)^{i+j}$). We
sanity-check it reproduces @eq-det2.
Expand the $2\times2$ matrix along the first row ($i=1$):
$$
\det\begin{bmatrix}a&b\\c&d\end{bmatrix}
= (-1)^{1+1}a\,M_{11} + (-1)^{1+2}b\,M_{12}
= a\cdot d - b\cdot c = ad-bc,
$$
where $M_{11}=\det[d]=d$ (delete row 1, col 1) and $M_{12}=\det[c]=c$ (delete row 1, col 2).
It matches. The checkerboard of signs $(-1)^{i+j}$ — $+,-,+,\dots$ — is the alternating
property bookkept into the recursion. The cofactor expansion is good for $2\times2$ and
$3\times3$ by hand and for sparse or structured matrices; for anything dense and larger,
elimination (@sec-laws) is the right tool.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the determinant on real, honest matrices and tasks — never a toy rigged to flatter.
The first we carry through by hand; the rest show where the determinant genuinely earns its
keep and where it does not.

## Example 1 — a $3\times3$ determinant by hand, and what it says {#sec-ex-byhand}

Consider the map
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix},
$$
a symmetric matrix of the kind that shows up everywhere as a covariance or Gram matrix. We
ask: is it invertible, and by how much does it scale 3-D volume? Expand along the first row
using @thm-cofactor:
$$
\det A = 2\cdot M_{11} - 1\cdot M_{12} + 0\cdot M_{13},
$$
with signs $+,-,+$. The third term dies because $A_{13}=0$, so we need only two minors:
$$
M_{11} = \det\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = 3\cdot 2 - 1\cdot 1 = 5,
\qquad
M_{12} = \det\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = 1\cdot 2 - 1\cdot 0 = 2.
$$
Therefore
$$
\det A = 2(5) - 1(2) + 0 = 10 - 2 = 8 .
$$
The determinant is $8\neq 0$, so $A$ is invertible (@thm-singular): the columns are
independent, the map can be undone, $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has a unique solution for every
$\mathbf{b}$. And the geometric reading: this map *inflates 3-D volume eightfold*. The unit
cube becomes a parallelepiped of volume $8$. Let us confirm with elimination (@sec-laws) and
numerically, watching the diagonal product emerge.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-ex1-det
#| echo: true
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[2.0, 1.0, 0.0],
              [1.0, 3.0, 1.0],
              [0.0, 1.0, 2.0]])

print("det A (library)      =", round(np.linalg.det(A), 10))   # expect 8

# Reproduce the by-hand cofactor result.
M11 = A[1, 1] * A[2, 2] - A[1, 2] * A[2, 1]
M12 = A[1, 0] * A[2, 2] - A[1, 2] * A[2, 0]
det_cofactor = A[0, 0] * M11 - A[0, 1] * M12
print("det A (cofactor row1)=", det_cofactor)

# Reproduce via LU: det = sign(P) * product of U's diagonal.
import scipy.linalg as sla
P, L, U = sla.lu(A)
sign = np.linalg.det(P)                       # +-1 from the row swaps
print("U diagonal           =", np.round(np.diag(U), 4))
print("sign(P)*prod(diagU)  =", round(sign * np.prod(np.diag(U)), 10))

# The inverse's determinant is the reciprocal (eq-det-inv).
print("det(A^-1)            =", round(np.linalg.det(np.linalg.inv(A)), 6),
      " vs 1/8 =", 1/8)
print("det(A^T)             =", round(np.linalg.det(A.T), 10), " (= det A, thm-transpose)")
```

Every cross-check lands: the cofactor arithmetic, the LU diagonal product, the reciprocal
rule $\det(A^{-1})=1/8$ from @eq-det-inv, and the transpose rule $\det(A^\top)=\det A$ from
@thm-transpose. The math was done by hand; the code is the microscope.

## Example 2 — the determinant *is* the change-of-variables factor for densities {#sec-jacobian}

This is the example where the determinant does indispensable work in data science, and it is
the hook the Probability track collects. Suppose a random vector $\mathbf{z}$ has a known
probability density $p_{\mathbf{z}}$, and we transform it linearly, $\mathbf{x}=A\mathbf{z}$
with $A$ invertible. What is the density of $\mathbf{x}$?

Probability mass must be conserved: the chance of landing in a region $R$ of
$\mathbf{x}$-space equals the chance of landing in the pre-image $A^{-1}R$ of
$\mathbf{z}$-space. In integral form, $\int_R p_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x})\,d\mathbf{x} =
\int_{A^{-1}R} p_{\mathbf{z}}(\mathbf{z})\,d\mathbf{z}$. To compare the two integrands we must
account for how the linear map stretches volume — and *that factor is exactly the
determinant*. A tiny volume $d\mathbf{z}$ in $\mathbf{z}$-space becomes a volume
$|\det A|\,d\mathbf{z}$ in $\mathbf{x}$-space (the Core's "same factor everywhere"
property). Substituting $\mathbf{z}=A^{-1}\mathbf{x}$, so $d\mathbf{z}=|\det
A^{-1}|\,d\mathbf{x}=\frac{1}{|\det A|}d\mathbf{x}$ by @eq-det-inv, gives the change-of-
variables formula for a linear map:
$$
p_{\mathbf{x}}(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{|\det A|}\;p_{\mathbf{z}}\!\big(A^{-1}\mathbf{x}\big).
$$ {#eq-cov}
The $\frac{1}{|\det A|}$ is doing essential work: if $A$ stretches space (volume factor
$|\det A|>1$), the same probability mass is spread over a larger region, so the density must
*drop* by exactly that factor to keep the total at $1$. @fig-jacobian shows this on a 2-D
Gaussian cloud: the map stretches the cloud, its area grows by $|\det A|$, and the per-point
density falls correspondingly.

```{python}
#| label: fig-jacobian
#| fig-cap: "The determinant as the density-rescaling factor. **Left:** $n=4000$ samples from a standard 2-D Gaussian $\\mathbf{z}$, with the unit square (area $1$) marked. **Right:** the transformed cloud $\\mathbf{x}=A\\mathbf{z}$ for $A=\\bigl[\\begin{smallmatrix}1.8&0.9\\\\0&1.3\\end{smallmatrix}\\bigr]$ ($\\det A=2.34$); the unit square has become a parallelogram of area $2.34$. The cloud spreads over $2.34\\times$ the area, so its density at any point falls to $1/2.34$ of the original — exactly the factor in @eq-cov. This is the linear special case of the general Jacobian; the full random-vector treatment is [PROB-18]."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
Z = rng.normal(size=(2, 4000))
A = np.array([[1.8, 0.9], [0.0, 1.3]])
X = A @ Z
detA = np.linalg.det(A)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))
axL.scatter(Z[0], Z[1], s=4, color=VEC, alpha=0.25)
sq = Polygon([(0, 0), (1, 0), (1, 1), (0, 1)], closed=True, fill=False,
             edgecolor=ACCENT, lw=2.2)
axL.add_patch(sq)
axL.set_title(r"$\mathbf{z}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I)$; unit square area 1")
axL.set_xlim(-4, 4); axL.set_ylim(-4, 4); axL.set_aspect("equal")
axL.set_xlabel("$z_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$z_2$")

axR.scatter(X[0], X[1], s=4, color=HILITE, alpha=0.25)
c1, c2 = A[:, 0], A[:, 1]
para = Polygon([(0, 0), tuple(c1), tuple(c1 + c2), tuple(c2)], closed=True,
               fill=False, edgecolor=ACCENT, lw=2.2)
axR.add_patch(para)
axR.set_title(fr"$\mathbf{{x}}=A\mathbf{{z}}$; parallelogram area $=\mathrm{{det}}\,A={detA:.2f}$")
axR.set_xlim(-6, 6); axR.set_ylim(-6, 6); axR.set_aspect("equal")
axR.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"det A = {detA:.4f}  -> density rescales by 1/|det A| = {1/abs(detA):.4f}")
```

This is the determinant's most important appearance in statistics: it is why the
multivariate normal density carries a $|\det\Sigma|^{-1/2}$ factor, why normalizing flows
track a "log-determinant of the Jacobian" term, and why any change of variables in a density
must multiply by the Jacobian determinant. The geometry — *volume scaling* — is the entire
content; the probabilistic packaging is [PROB-18]'s to own, and the nonlinear Jacobian
(where $A$ is replaced by a local linearization, the matrix of partial derivatives) is built
in the calculus movement of this track ([GEOM-22]).

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where does the $|\det\Sigma|^{-1/2}$ in the multivariate Gaussian come from?"* From exactly
@eq-cov. A standard Gaussian $\mathbf{z}\sim\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},I)$ pushed through a linear
map $\mathbf{x}=A\mathbf{z}+\boldsymbol\mu$ has covariance $\Sigma=AA^\top$, so
$\det\Sigma=\det(AA^\top)=(\det A)^2$ by the product and transpose rules — hence
$|\det A|=|\det\Sigma|^{1/2}$. The change-of-variables factor $1/|\det A|$ becomes
$1/|\det\Sigma|^{1/2}=|\det\Sigma|^{-1/2}$, the normalizing constant that keeps the Gaussian
integrating to $1$. Being able to trace that factor back to "volume scaling of the linear
map" is the answer that separates memorization from understanding.
:::

## Example 3 — orientation and the sign, on a reflection {#sec-ex-sign}

The sign of the determinant is the part most often forgotten, so we make it concrete. A
**rotation** preserves handedness and has $\det = +1$; a **reflection** flips handedness and
has $\det=-1$. Both preserve area ($|\det|=1$ — they are rigid), but they differ in sign,
and the sign is the only thing distinguishing "rotate" from "rotate-then-mirror."

```{python}
#| label: fig-orientation
#| fig-cap: "The sign is orientation. A small asymmetric 'F' shape (so we can see flips) under three maps. **Left:** a $40^\\circ$ rotation, $\\det=+1$ — the F turns but is not mirrored. **Middle:** a reflection across the $y$-axis, $\\det=-1$ — the F is mirror-imaged (note it now reads backwards). **Right:** a shear, $\\det=+1$ — area and orientation preserved, only slanted. Magnitude $|\\det|$ is the area factor (all $1$ here); the *sign* records whether a right-handed frame stayed right-handed."
# An asymmetric polyline shaped like 'F'.
F = np.array([[0, 0], [0, 3], [1.6, 3], [1.6, 2.4], [0.6, 2.4],
              [0.6, 1.7], [1.3, 1.7], [1.3, 1.1], [0.6, 1.1], [0.6, 0], [0, 0]]).T
F = F - F.mean(1, keepdims=True)   # center it

theta = np.deg2rad(40)
R = np.array([[np.cos(theta), -np.sin(theta)], [np.sin(theta), np.cos(theta)]])  # rotation
Ref = np.array([[-1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0]])                                        # reflection
Sh = np.array([[1.0, 0.8], [0.0, 1.0]])                                          # shear

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.4, 3.6))
for ax, (M, name) in zip(axes, [(R, "rotation"), (Ref, "reflection"), (Sh, "shear")]):
    MF = M @ F
    ax.plot(F[0], F[1], color=GREY, lw=1.4, ls="--", label="original")
    ax.plot(MF[0], MF[1], color=HILITE, lw=2.4, label="image")
    ax.set_title(f"{name}:  det = {np.linalg.det(M):+.0f}")
    ax.set_xlim(-3, 3); ax.set_ylim(-3, 3); ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.legend(loc="lower left", fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
print("det(rotation) =", round(np.linalg.det(R), 3),
      " det(reflection) =", round(np.linalg.det(Ref), 3),
      " det(shear) =", round(np.linalg.det(Sh), 3))
```

The reflection's $\det=-1$ is the algebra noticing that the image F reads backwards. In data
work this matters whenever orientation carries meaning — for instance, a "rotation" matrix
estimated from noisy data that secretly contains a reflection ($\det=-1$) will silently flip
your coordinate frame, a bug the sign of the determinant catches at a glance.

## Example 4 — the edge of applicability: $\det\approx 0$ is a bad singularity test {#sec-ex-edge}

Here is the honest boundary, and it is the most important practical lesson in the article.
The clean theorem $\det A = 0 \Leftrightarrow$ singular is **exactly true in exact
arithmetic** and **dangerously misleading on a computer with finite precision**. The reason
is that the determinant *scales with the entries*, so its raw magnitude tells you almost
nothing about how close a matrix is to singular.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bad-test
#| fig-cap: "Why $\\det\\approx 0$ fails as a singularity test. **Left:** a perfectly well-behaved, easily invertible matrix $0.1\\cdot I_n$ — its inverse is just $10\\cdot I_n$ — yet its determinant is $0.1^n$, which underflows toward zero as $n$ grows (red). A tiny determinant here means *nothing is wrong*. **Right:** a genuinely near-singular matrix family whose determinant stays moderate while its condition number (blue, log scale) explodes — the determinant fails to raise the alarm. The honest near-singularity diagnostic is the condition number $\\kappa$ (the smallest-to-largest singular-value ratio), not $\\det$ ([GEOM-26])."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.8))

# Left: well-conditioned but tiny determinant.
ns = np.arange(2, 26)
dets = 0.1 ** ns               # det(0.1 * I_n) = 0.1^n
axL.semilogy(ns, dets, color=HILITE, marker="o", ms=3)
axL.set_xlabel("dimension $n$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$\mathrm{det}(0.1\,I_n)$")
axL.set_title("Perfectly invertible, yet det $\\to 0$")

# Right: near-singular, but det stays moderate while kappa explodes.
eps = np.logspace(-1, -9, 30)
det_vals, kappa_vals = [], []
for e in eps:
    M = np.array([[1.0, 1.0], [1.0, 1.0 + e]])   # nearly singular as e -> 0
    det_vals.append(np.linalg.det(M))
    kappa_vals.append(np.linalg.cond(M))
axR.loglog(eps, np.abs(det_vals), color=HILITE, marker="o", ms=3, label=r"$|\mathrm{det}|$")
axR.loglog(eps, kappa_vals, color=VEC, marker="s", ms=3, label=r"$\kappa$ (cond. number)")
axR.set_xlabel(r"perturbation $\epsilon$ (smaller = nearer singular)")
axR.set_title("det and condition number disagree")
axR.legend(fontsize=9); axR.grid(True, which="both", alpha=0.2)
axR.invert_xaxis()
plt.tight_layout()
print("det(0.1*I_20) =", 0.1**20, " (tiny, but 0.1*I_20 is trivially invertible)")
print("near-singular [[1,1],[1,1.001]]: det =", round(np.linalg.det([[1,1],[1,1.001]]), 6),
      " cond =", round(np.linalg.cond([[1,1],[1,1.001]]), 1))
```

The left panel is the killer counterexample: $0.1\cdot I_n$ is the easiest matrix in the
world to invert (multiply by $10$), yet its determinant $0.1^n$ races toward zero as $n$
grows and underflows to literal $0.0$ in floating point around $n=300$ — at which point a
"$\det\approx 0$" test would falsely declare a trivially invertible matrix singular. The
right panel shows the dual failure: scaling can keep $|\det|$ moderate even as the matrix
becomes genuinely ill-conditioned. **The determinant conflates "near-singular" with "small
entries," and those are completely different things.** The correct, scale-aware diagnostic
is the **condition number** $\kappa(A)=\sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ (the ratio of largest to
smallest singular value), which is invariant to overall scaling and measures exactly how
close to collapse the map is. We build it in [GEOM-26]; flag it now as the tool that
*replaces* the determinant for any numerical singularity question.

# Failure modes and what to reach for instead {#sec-failure}

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Never test numerical singularity with $\det A\approx 0$.** Three independent reasons, each
fatal on its own: (1) the determinant scales as $\sigma_1\sigma_2\cdots\sigma_n$, so it can
be astronomically small for a perfectly well-conditioned matrix (just small entries, as with
$0.1\,I_n$) or not-small for a nearly singular one; (2) it overflows or underflows for even
moderate $n$, becoming a meaningless $0$ or $\infty$ in floating point; (3) it gives no
*degree* of singularity — a single near-zero answer cannot tell you whether you are slightly
or catastrophically ill-conditioned. The right diagnostic is the **condition number** (the
singular-value ratio, [GEOM-26]) or directly the **smallest singular value**. If you only
need to *solve* a system, don't compute $\det$ at all — factor and back-substitute ([GEOM-11]),
and let the solver's pivoting warn you about near-singularity.
:::

A few more honest boundaries:

- **The determinant says *whether*, not *how*, a matrix is singular.** $\det A=0$ tells you
  the columns are dependent but not *which* dependence holds or how many dimensions
  collapsed. For that you need the null space ([GEOM-12]) or the SVD ([GEOM-20]); the
  determinant is the alarm, not the diagnosis. (It is also blind to *rank*: every singular
  matrix has $\det=0$, whether its rank is $n-1$ or $0$.)

- **Cofactor expansion is computationally hopeless.** The recursive definition is $O(n!)$;
  a $20\times20$ determinant by cofactors would take longer than the age of the universe.
  Always reduce to LU/triangular form ($O(n^3)$), and prefer working with $\log|\det|$ (the
  sum of $\log|U_{ii}|$) to dodge overflow when the determinant is genuinely needed (as in a
  Gaussian log-likelihood).

- **Where the determinant is genuinely the right tool.** Small symbolic or exact matrices
  ($2\times2$, $3\times3$, structured ones); *theoretical* arguments about invertibility and
  orientation; the **characteristic polynomial** $\det(A-\lambda I)$ that defines eigenvalues
  ([GEOM-14]); the **change-of-variables Jacobian** for densities (@sec-jacobian, [PROB-18]);
  Cramer's rule (elegant on paper, never used numerically); and the *volume / orientation*
  questions it was invented for. Outside those, reach for rank, condition number, or a
  factorization.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you actually compute a determinant in a data-science pipeline, and when would
you refuse?"* The honest summary:

- **Compute it** when you need a *volume or orientation* answer: the log-determinant term in
  a multivariate Gaussian or normalizing-flow log-likelihood (always as $\log|\det|$ via the
  LU diagonal, never the raw product); checking whether an estimated transform contains a
  reflection ($\det<0$); the characteristic polynomial for a small eigenproblem by hand.
- **Refuse it** for testing invertibility or near-singularity of a numerical matrix — use the
  condition number or smallest singular value ([GEOM-26]); for solving $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$
  — factor and solve ([GEOM-11]); and never via cofactors for anything beyond $3\times3$.
- **The one-line judgment:** the determinant is a superb *theoretical* invariant and a poor
  *numerical* diagnostic. Know which question you are asking, and pick the tool to match.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-11 *Solving $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$*]** and **[GEOM-12 *Rank and
the Four Fundamental Subspaces*]**, which together established *that* a square matrix is
invertible exactly when it has full rank — when nothing collapses. This article supplied the
single number that detects the collapse, $\det A$, and revealed it to be the **signed volume
scaling factor** of the map. Every algebraic law turned out to be a sentence about volume.
From here the track continues:

- **[GEOM-14 *Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors*]** — the determinant's biggest theoretical job:
  the **characteristic polynomial** $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$ is the equation whose roots are the
  eigenvalues, and it is well-defined precisely because similarity preserves the determinant
  (@thm-product). A zero eigenvalue ($\lambda=0$ a root) means $\det A=0$ — the
  rank-deficiency of [GEOM-12], seen through the determinant.
- **[GEOM-20 *The Singular Value Decomposition*]** — makes $|\det A|=\sigma_1\sigma_2\cdots
  \sigma_n$ (the product of singular values) explicit, which is exactly why the determinant
  is a bad near-singularity test (@sec-ex-edge): it is a *product*, blind to whether one
  factor is dangerously small.
- **[GEOM-26 *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*]** — the scale-aware successor to the
  determinant for the only question $\det$ tempts you to misuse it on: *how close to singular
  is this matrix, numerically?*

Downstream of the track, the **change-of-variables Jacobian** of @sec-jacobian is the hook
that **[PROB-18 *Linear Transforms of Random Vectors*]** collects, where it becomes the
$|\det\Sigma|^{-1/2}$ of the multivariate normal and the engine of density transformation.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The origin.** Gabriel Cramer's 1750 *Introduction à l'analyse des lignes courbes
  algébriques* (Cramer's rule) and Augustin-Louis Cauchy's 1812 memoir, which fixed the term
  "déterminant" and proved the product rule $\det(AB)=\det A\det B$ — the determinant as a
  *solvability detector* first, a *volume* second.
- **The properties-first definition.** The "multilinear, alternating, normalized" characterization
  of @def-det-properties, and the proof that it forces a unique function, is the modern
  (Weierstrass/Artin) treatment; Sheldon Axler's *Linear Algebra Done Right* and Michael
  Artin's *Algebra* both develop the determinant this way, geometry first.
- **The numerical verdict.** Any text on numerical linear algebra (Trefethen & Bau, *Numerical
  Linear Algebra*; Golub & Van Loan, *Matrix Computations*) makes the case of @sec-ex-edge
  rigorously: never use $\det$ for conditioning; use the SVD's singular values.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *Why is the characteristic polynomial's constant
  term $\pm\det A$ and its leading behavior tied to the trace?* — eigenvalue structure,
  [GEOM-14]. *What is the determinant of a non-square or rank-deficient matrix?* — there
  isn't one; the right generalization is the product of singular values / the SVD,
  [GEOM-20]–[GEOM-21]. *How does the volume factor generalize when the map is nonlinear?* —
  the Jacobian determinant of the local linearization, built in [GEOM-22].
